title: Reserved identifiers should not be defined or declared
description: Reserved identifiers should not be defined or declaredAnalyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode Smellbased-on-misrabad-practicecertWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoDefining or declaring identifiers with reserved names may lead to undefined behavior. Therefore, reserved names should not be used as identifiers.This rule applies to:definedidentifiers that contain two consecutive underscoresidentifiers that begin with an underscore, followed by an uppercase letteridentifiers in the global namespace that start with an underscoreAvailable In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise CIDeveloper EditionAvailable Since9.1©2025SonarSource Sàrl. All rights
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
int __reserved_name;     // Noncompliant - double underscore
int _Reserved_name;       // Noncompliant - underscore + uppercase
int _global_var;          // Noncompliant - global scope with leading underscore
#define __MY_MACRO 1      // Noncompliant - double underscore
void __my_function();     // Noncompliant - double underscore
compliant:
int my_variable;         // Compliant - no reserved pattern
int reserved_name;        // Compliant - no leading underscore
static int _local_var;    // Compliant - not in global scope
#define MY_MACRO 1        // Compliant - no double underscore
void my_function();       // Compliant - proper naming

checks.message: Reserved identifier with double underscore should not be defined or declared
Reserved identifier beginning with underscore followed by uppercase letter should not be used
Global identifier starting with underscore violates reserved naming convention
Use of reserved identifier pattern violates C naming conventions and may cause undefined behavior
